Agentic Software Bug Assistant

Source: vignettes/software_bug_assistant.Rmd
software_bug_assistant.Rmd

Introduction

This vignette demonstrates the Software Bug Assistant pattern using HydraR. In this scenario, two agent nodes work together to resolve a software bug: 1. Bug Analyzer Node: Reviews the error message and current code, then proposes a patch. 2. Test Runner Node: Simulates running a test suite against the proposed patch. If the test fails, it provides error traces back to the Bug Analyzer.

This is a classic “Generate-and-Test” self-healing loop.

Setup 

library(HydraR)

Building the DAG

Initialize the AgentDAG.

dag <- AgentDAG$new()

1. The Bug Analyzer Node

This node mocks an LLM digesting a bug report and iterative test failures to eventually produce a working patch.

analyzer_node <- AgentLogicNode$new(id = "Analyzer", logic_fn = function(state, memory = NULL) {
  # Extract state
  bug_report <- state$get("bug_report")
  test_feedback <- state$get("test_feedback")

  attempts <- state$get("patch_attempts")
  if (is.null(attempts)) attempts <- 0
  attempts <- attempts + 1

  # Mock behavior: It takes 2 tries to fix a Null Pointer Exception
  proposed_patch <- if (attempts == 1) {
    "if (x == NULL) return 0;" # Close, but maybe not quite right
  } else {
    "if (is.null(x)) return(0)" # Correct R syntax
  }

  list(
    status = "SUCCESS",
    output = list(
      proposed_patch = proposed_patch,
      patch_attempts = attempts
    )
  )
})

dag$add_node(analyzer_node)

2. The Test Runner Node

This node evaluates the patch. We’ll simply check if the proposed patch is exactly the “correct R syntax” string.

tester_node <- AgentLogicNode$new(id = "Tester", logic_fn = function(state, memory = NULL) {
  patch <- state$get("proposed_patch")

  if (patch == "if (is.null(x)) return(0)") {
    list(
      status = "SUCCESS",
      output = list(
        tests_passed = TRUE,
        test_feedback = "All 5 tests passed successfully."
      )
    )
  } else {
    list(
      status = "SUCCESS",
      output = list(
        tests_passed = FALSE,
        test_feedback = "Error: object 'NULL' not found. Did you mean to use 'is.null()'?"
      )
    )
  }
})

dag$add_node(tester_node)

Defining Transitions

We structure the loop. Tester conditionally loops back to Analyzer upon test failure.

dag$set_start_node("Analyzer")

dag$add_edge("Analyzer", "Tester")

dag$add_conditional_edge(
  from = "Tester",
  test = function(out) {
    isTRUE(out$tests_passed)
  },
  if_true = NULL, # End execution
  if_false = "Analyzer" # Retry
)

compiled_dag <- dag$compile()
#> Graph compiled successfully.

Running the Scenario

Provide the initial bug report and start the DAG.

initial_state <- list(
  bug_report = "Function crashes when 'x' is missing."
)

cat("Starting Automatic Bug Remediation...\n")
#> Starting Automatic Bug Remediation...
result <- compiled_dag$run(initial_state = initial_state, max_steps = 10)
#> Graph compiled successfully.
#> [Iteration 1] Running Node: Analyzer
#>    [Analyzer] Executing R logic...
#> [Iteration 2] Running Node: Tester
#>    [Tester] Executing R logic...
#> [Iteration 3] Running Node: Analyzer
#>    [Analyzer] Executing R logic...
#> [Iteration 4] Running Node: Tester
#>    [Tester] Executing R logic...

cat("\n--- RESOLUTION RESULT ---\n")
#> 
#> --- RESOLUTION RESULT ---
cat("Patch Attempts:", result$state$get("patch_attempts"), "\n")
#> Patch Attempts: 2
cat("Final Patch:", result$state$get("proposed_patch"), "\n")
#> Final Patch: if (is.null(x)) return(0)
cat("Test Results:", result$state$get("test_feedback"), "\n")
#> Test Results: All 5 tests passed successfully.

The DAG correctly detected the test failure, routed the detailed feedback back to the analyzer, and produced a successful patch on its second attempt!
